seo.title: Cybersecurity AI Coding Assistant AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for ai coding assistant AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying an AI coding assistant in a cybersecurity environment introduces unique risks where the same tool designed to accelerate secure development can become an attack surface itself — leaking threat intelligence, generating vulnerable code, or triggering automated responses that escalate incidents. This checklist ensures your AI coding agent operates within NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 boundaries while maintaining the integrity of your security operations. Every item is scoped to the specific intersection of code-aware LLM agents and cybersecurity workflows where failure consequences range from adversary exploitation to classified data breaches.

sections.heading: Data Isolation & Threat Intelligence Protection
sections.description: Prevent the AI coding agent from inadvertently exposing threat intelligence, IOCs, CVE details, or classified adversary TTPs through LLM API calls, prompt injection, or model memorization pathways.

items.label: Classify and tag threat intelligence data before repository ingestion
items.description: Apply data classification labels (e.g., TLP:RED, TLP:AMBER) to all files containing IOCs, adversary TTPs, or internal vulnerability data before they enter the AI agent's repository context window. Enforce tag-based access controls so the LLM cannot include classified content in prompts sent to external APIs.

items.label: Enforce outbound prompt sanitization for external LLM API calls
items.description: Implement a prompt sanitization layer that scans all outbound API payloads for patterns matching CVE IDs, IP addresses, domain indicators, hash values, and internal system names before transmission to any third-party LLM provider. Block and alert on any match.

items.label: Deploy a self-hosted or private LLM endpoint for sensitive codebases
items.description: For repositories containing security tooling, detection rules, or exploit research, route all AI coding assistant requests to an on-premises or VPC-isolated LLM deployment (e.g., Azure OpenAI with private endpoints) rather than shared public APIs to eliminate data exfiltration risk.

items.label: Restrict repository context scope to need-to-know file paths
items.description: Configure the code-aware agent to only index and retrieve files explicitly scoped to the current task. Prevent the agent from autonomously expanding its context to include threat intelligence feeds, SIEM rule repositories, or incident response playbooks unless explicitly authorized per session.

items.label: Audit all LLM prompt and completion logs for data leakage indicators
items.description: Enable full prompt/completion logging to an immutable SIEM-integrated store and run automated DLP scans against logs daily. Flag any completions that reference internal hostnames, vulnerability details not in public CVE databases, or internal network topology.

items.label: Implement session-level data retention limits for AI context windows
items.description: Enforce automatic context window purging after each coding session ends. Ensure no threat intelligence, credentials, or internal security architecture details persist in agent memory, vector stores, or embedding caches between sessions.

sections.heading: Secure Code Generation & Vulnerability Prevention
sections.description: Ensure the AI coding assistant produces code that meets security standards, does not introduce vulnerabilities, and is validated against authoritative sources before integration into security tooling or production systems.

items.label: Integrate SAST scanning on every AI-generated code block before commit
items.description: Automatically pipe all AI-generated code through a static analysis tool (e.g., Semgrep, Bandit, CodeQL) configured with security-specific rulesets before allowing any commit. Fail the pipeline on any HIGH or CRITICAL severity finding and require human review before override.

items.label: Scan AI-generated code for hardcoded credentials and secrets
items.description: Run dedicated secret detection tools (e.g., Trufflehog, GitLeaks, Detect-Secrets) against every AI-generated file and diff. Configure pre-commit hooks and CI gates to block any code containing API keys, passwords, tokens, or private key material regardless of whether the AI or human authored it.

items.label: Validate all AI-suggested CVE remediations against the NVD and vendor advisories
items.description: When the AI coding assistant recommends a patch or mitigation for a known vulnerability, require automated cross-referencing against the NIST NVD API and relevant vendor security advisories before the fix is accepted. Flag discrepancies where AI guidance contradicts official CVE remediation steps.

items.label: Enforce dependency safety checks on all AI-recommended package additions
items.description: Run every AI-suggested dependency through OSV Scanner, Snyk, or OWASP Dependency-Check before installation. Verify the package against known malicious package databases and confirm the suggested version is not affected by any active CVE with a CVSS score above 7.0.

items.label: Execute AI-generated code exclusively in isolated sandbox environments
items.description: Configure the agent's sandbox execution environment with no network egress, read-only filesystem mounts outside the working directory, and resource limits. Never allow AI-generated code to execute directly on production systems, security appliances, or systems with access to live threat intelligence feeds.

items.label: Enforce license compliance scanning on all AI-generated and suggested code
items.description: Run FOSSA, REUSE, or equivalent license compliance tooling against all AI-generated code to detect GPL, AGPL, or other copyleft licenses that may conflict with your security tooling's distribution requirements. Block merges where license conflicts are detected.

items.label: Validate AI-generated security tool logic against known adversary evasion techniques
items.description: For AI-generated detection rules, YARA signatures, or security scripts, run adversarial validation tests using known evasion samples from threat intelligence feeds. Confirm the generated logic does not contain logical flaws that adversaries could exploit to bypass detection.

sections.heading: Human Oversight & Automated Action Controls
sections.description: Establish mandatory human approval gates for any AI-recommended or AI-initiated actions that could affect critical systems, trigger incident response workflows, or modify security configurations.

items.label: Require human approval for all AI-recommended changes to critical security systems
items.description: Define a list of critical system categories (firewalls, EDR configurations, SIEM rules, IAM policies, network segmentation rules) and enforce a mandatory human approval workflow before any AI-generated code or configuration change is applied to these systems. Implement this as a hard gate in your CI/CD pipeline with named approver requirements.

items.label: Implement a kill switch to immediately halt all AI agent automated actions
items.description: Deploy an emergency stop mechanism accessible to SOC analysts and security leads that immediately suspends all AI agent execution, queues pending actions for human review, and sends alerts to the security team. Test this kill switch quarterly as part of incident response drills.

items.label: Log all AI agent actions with immutable audit trails linked to approving personnel
items.description: Ensure every action taken or recommended by the AI coding agent is logged with a timestamp, the agent's reasoning, the specific code or configuration change, and the identity of the human approver. Store logs in a WORM-compliant system and retain for a minimum of 12 months per SOC 2 Type II requirements.

items.label: Define and enforce blast radius limits for AI agent execution permissions
items.description: Apply least-privilege principles to the AI agent's service account and execution context. The agent should only have write access to designated development branches, never to main/production branches, and should have zero access to production infrastructure APIs, secrets managers, or deployment pipelines without explicit per-session elevation.

items.label: Establish escalation thresholds that automatically pause AI agent activity during active incidents
items.description: Integrate the AI coding agent with your SIEM or SOAR platform so that when an active security incident is declared above a defined severity threshold, the agent automatically suspends all automated code execution and deployment activities until the incident is resolved and a human explicitly re-enables the agent.

items.label: Conduct quarterly red team exercises targeting AI agent manipulation and prompt injection
items.description: Schedule adversarial testing sessions where your red team attempts to manipulate the AI coding agent through malicious code comments, crafted repository content, or prompt injection via test files. Document findings and remediate any identified manipulation pathways before the next deployment cycle.

sections.heading: Compliance Monitoring & Continuous Assurance
sections.description: Maintain ongoing compliance with NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and CISA directives through continuous monitoring, evidence collection, and periodic assessments specific to AI agent behavior in security environments.

items.label: Map AI agent controls to NIST CSF functions and document coverage gaps
items.description: Create and maintain a control mapping document that aligns each AI agent safeguard to the relevant NIST CSF function (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover). Identify and formally accept or remediate any coverage gaps, particularly in the Detect and Respond functions where AI errors carry the highest consequence.

items.label: Collect and retain SOC 2 Type II evidence for all AI agent access and change events
items.description: Configure automated evidence collection for SOC 2 Type II audit requirements including access logs, change management records, approval workflows, and exception reports generated by the AI coding agent. Ensure evidence is collected continuously and stored in a format acceptable to your external auditor.

items.label: Perform ISO 27001 Annex A control reviews for AI-specific risk scenarios quarterly
items.description: Conduct quarterly reviews of ISO 27001 Annex A controls most relevant to AI coding agents, specifically A.8 (Asset Management), A.9 (Access Control), A.12 (Operations Security), and A.14 (System Acquisition). Document findings in your ISMS and update risk treatment plans accordingly.

items.label: Monitor for and respond to CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities in AI agent dependencies
items.description: Subscribe to CISA KEV catalog updates and implement automated alerting when any dependency used by the AI coding agent or its infrastructure appears in the KEV catalog. Establish a maximum 72-hour remediation SLA for KEV-listed vulnerabilities in the agent's dependency chain.

items.label: Conduct AI model behavior drift assessments after every major model update
items.description: After any update to the underlying LLM model version, run a standardized battery of security-specific test prompts to detect behavioral drift. Compare outputs against baseline benchmarks for credential handling, vulnerability disclosure, and code quality. Halt deployment if drift exceeds defined thresholds.

items.label: Publish and review an AI agent risk register updated at minimum monthly
items.description: Maintain a dedicated risk register for the AI coding agent that documents identified risks, likelihood, impact, current controls, residual risk, and risk owners. Review and update this register monthly with input from security engineering, compliance, and SOC leadership.

items.label: Validate AI agent vendor security posture annually via third-party assessment or SOC 2 report review
items.description: Obtain and review the SOC 2 Type II report or equivalent third-party security assessment for your AI coding assistant vendor annually. Verify their controls address data handling, model training data isolation, API security, and incident notification obligations relevant to your threat intelligence data.

notes.note: The NIST CSF 2.0 Govern function now explicitly requires organizations to establish policies for AI and automated tool risk management. Your AI coding agent must be included in your cybersecurity risk governance program with documented roles, responsibilities, and oversight mechanisms. The Detect function requires that AI agent anomalies — such as unexpected data access patterns or unusual code generation behavior — be treated as cybersecurity events subject to the same detection and response processes as other security incidents.

SOC 2 Type II auditors are increasingly scrutinizing AI tool usage under the Common Criteria related to logical access (CC6) and change management (CC8). You must demonstrate that the AI coding agent's access to source code repositories is governed by the same access control policies as human developers, that all AI-generated changes go through your change management process, and that you have monitoring controls capable of detecting unauthorized or anomalous AI agent behavior over the full audit period.

ISO 27001:2022 introduced new controls under Annex A specifically relevant to AI coding agents: A.8.25 (Secure development life cycle) requires that AI-generated code be subject to the same security review processes as human-written code, and A.5.23 (Information security for use of cloud services) requires formal risk assessment of cloud-based LLM APIs used by the agent. Additionally, A.8.8 (Management of technical vulnerabilities) must explicitly cover vulnerabilities introduced by AI-generated code and AI agent dependencies.

CISA's Secure by Design principles and Binding Operational Directives (BODs) require federal agencies and critical infrastructure operators to ensure that AI tools used in software development do not introduce known exploitable vulnerabilities. BOD 22-01 obligations extend to vulnerabilities in AI agent infrastructure and dependencies. CISA's AI security guidance specifically warns against deploying AI coding assistants with access to sensitive security configurations without human-in-the-loop controls, directly aligning with the human approval requirements in this checklist.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI coding agent against this checklist, automatically collect SOC 2 and ISO 27001 evidence, and receive real-time alerts when your cybersecurity AI deployment drifts out of compliance.
